cases.title: Emergency Department Waiting-Room Aggression
cases.body:
# Emergency Department Waiting-Room Aggression

prev_id: null

## Context

A hospital emergency department is trying to reduce hostility toward staff in the
waiting room. The worst incidents need security or clinical escalation, but many
lower-level conflicts build from the same background conditions: pain, fear,
long waits, crowded rooms, and a process that patients and visitors cannot see.

Triage makes the queue morally confusing from the waiting-room chair. Someone who
arrived later may be called first. A patient may disappear through one doorway
and then wait somewhere else. Tests, reviews, admissions, discharges, and
handoffs happen behind the scenes. Staff know the process is moving; patients
often experience only delay and silence.

The department cannot become first-come-first-served. It cannot safely promise
exact treatment times. It also cannot depend on clinicians repeatedly leaving
clinical work to explain the system one person at a time.

What could the department change in the waiting-room experience so ordinary
confusion and frustration are less likely to harden into aggression, while
preserving clinical triage and staff time?

## Synopsis

An emergency department wants to reduce waiting-room hostility toward staff; most conflict grows from pain, long waits, crowding, and a triage process patients cannot see.

cases.title: When NPX Becomes A Permission Boundary
cases.body:
# When NPX Becomes A Permission Boundary

prev_id: null

## Context

Package registries grew up around dependency records. A maintainer publishes a
version, projects declare ranges, installers fetch tarballs, and downstream
tools check for known vulnerable versions. That model fits a world where humans
mostly install packages during deliberate development work.

The boundary is changing. A developer, agent, or remote sandbox can run an
unfamiliar package as an executable command with a single prompt. The approval
surface is often little more than a package name and version. It does not
explain whether the package is new, whether maintainership changed, whether the
tarball matches its repository, whether install scripts will run, or whether the
command expects filesystem, network, credential, or subprocess access.

The registry has started to add more trust signals. Package provenance, trusted
publishing, staged publishing, install-time controls, vulnerability scanning,
and dependency update bots all reduce parts of the problem. Security vendors can
also flag suspicious package behavior faster than a public vulnerability record
appears. But the execution decision is still fragmented across registry
metadata, local installer flags, human review, dependency PRs, and third-party
alerts.

The harder case is an untrusted or newly changed version before the ecosystem has
absorbed it. A release with low downloads and short lifetime may be reversible in
practice, but after it gains dependents, build cache entries, lockfile pins, and
automation trust, removal becomes more expensive. The same window is when an
agent is most likely to treat the package as a tool rather than a dependency.

The question is what the new permission boundary should be. If package execution
is remote code execution by default, what evidence should be assembled before a
command runs? Which low-exposure releases should stay reversible? Which signals
are strong enough to block an agent without making every install a manual
security review?

## Synopsis

Package registries were built for dependency records, but agents and sandboxes now run unfamiliar packages as executable commands on a single prompt, shifting where the permission boundary sits.

cases.title: The AI Campus That Cannot Just Plug In
cases.body:
# The AI Campus That Cannot Just Plug In

prev_id: null

## Context

In 2026, a frontier AI buildout is no longer only a chip-procurement problem. A
large training or inference campus needs accelerators, but it also needs a site
that can take enormous electrical load, cool dense racks, get permits, connect to
the grid, and operate on a schedule that does not match normal utility planning.

The numbers have moved fast. The International Energy Agency estimated data
centres used about 415 TWh of electricity in 2024, roughly 1.5% of global
electricity consumption, and projected that global data-centre electricity use
could reach about 945 TWh by 2030. Accelerated servers, driven mainly by AI, are
projected to grow much faster than conventional server demand.

The grid does not move like a GPU supply chain. Berkeley Lab's interconnection
queue data showed more than 2,060 GW of generation and storage capacity seeking
connection to the U.S. grid at the end of 2025, with many projects withdrawn and
completed projects taking longer to study and energize. A site can have capital,
land, and ordered hardware while still waiting on transmission upgrades,
substations, local opposition, water constraints, or a utility service agreement.

The behavior of large buyers has changed. Microsoft signed a 20-year agreement
with Constellation to support restarting Three Mile Island Unit 1 as the Crane
Clean Energy Center, adding roughly 835 MW of carbon-free power to the grid.
Google signed with Kairos Power for up to 500 MW of new 24/7 carbon-free nuclear
power, with the first unit targeted for 2030. Amazon has co-location and nuclear
development agreements tied to AWS data centers, including existing nuclear
capacity in Pennsylvania and small modular reactor projects in Washington and
Virginia.

For an AI lab, hyperscaler, utility, or data-center developer, the public story
still tends to fixate on chips and model releases. The siting meeting is more
physical. Which sites can actually be energized? Which megawatts are useful at
the hour and location where compute needs them? Who pays for the grid upgrades?
What happens if a model roadmap assumes capacity before the power system can
deliver it?

The decision is not simply "buy more electricity." The question is which input
actually slows deployment, how early it has to be secured, and who gains leverage
when compute demand runs into infrastructure built on a slower clock.

## Synopsis

A frontier AI campus is no longer just a chip-procurement problem: the binding constraints are electrical load, cooling, permits, and grid connection on a schedule utilities are not built for.

cases.title: The Publisher Whose Search Traffic Stopped Arriving
cases.body:
# The Publisher Whose Search Traffic Stopped Arriving

prev_id: null

## Context

A general-interest publisher enters 2026 with a familiar dashboard problem. The
site still ranks for many evergreen explainers. Google Search Console still shows
impressions. Some stories are still being cited or summarized in search results.
But sessions from classic search are weaker, and the decline is concentrated in
the pages that used to answer ordinary informational queries.

The search page itself has changed. Google AI Overviews and AI Mode, ChatGPT
Search, Perplexity, and similar products can now satisfy a user before the user
visits a publisher's site. Pew Research found in 2025 that Google users clicked a
traditional result in 8% of visits when an AI summary appeared, compared with 15%
when it did not. Clicks on links inside the AI summary were rarer. Google says
its AI features surface links and that no special markup is needed beyond normal
search eligibility, but the measurement is awkward: AI Overviews and AI Mode are
rolled into ordinary Search Console reporting rather than broken out as a clean
distribution channel.

The impact is not even. Industry data from 2026 showed large organic-click drops
for a portfolio of publishers after AI Overviews expanded, while breaking-news
traffic and Discover clicks rose. Other large-scale datasets showed a smaller
aggregate decline across the web, with sharper pain for some publisher classes
than for others. A newsroom can therefore be in real trouble even while someone
else can say search is not dead.

The publisher's internal argument gets messy quickly. The SEO team sees stable
rankings and wants technical fixes. The editorial team wants to protect service
journalism and evergreen explainers because those pages used to pay for less
commercial work. The ad team sees fewer pageviews. The subscription team cares
less about raw visits and more about readers who know the brand well enough to
return directly. The platform team cannot tell whether a citation inside an AI
answer is a substitute for a visit, a brand impression, or just invisible
extraction.

The publisher has to decide what kind of attention it is still earning, what
kind it has lost, and what it should build for next. A recipe database, a local
accountability investigation, a travel guide, a product review, and a breaking
news live blog may not be the same business anymore.

## Synopsis

A publisher's classic search traffic is eroding, concentrated in the evergreen explainers, as AI Overviews and answer engines satisfy informational queries on the results page itself.

cases.title: Airport Liquid Rule Congestion
cases.body:
# Airport Liquid Rule Congestion

prev_id: null

## Context

The airport security checkpoint has a recurring slowdown. Passengers arrive at
the X-ray belt with drink bottles, cosmetics, gels, or other containers that do
not comply with the carry-on liquid rule. By the time the problem is found, the
bag is already in the screening lane.

The interruption is small in isolation and ugly in aggregate. Staff stop the
flow, pull the bag aside, explain the rule, wait while the passenger searches,
argue about edge cases, discard items, or send the passenger away to repack. The
lane loses rhythm, the queue watches, and the passenger feels punished for a
mistake discovered too late.

The airport has already tried the obvious public-information layer: signs,
website copy, airline reminders, and announcements. They help some passengers
but not enough. The failures are concentrated among rushed travelers, families,
infrequent flyers, passengers using cabin bags only, and people who saw the rule
but did not translate it into an action before reaching the belt.

What could the airport change so fewer liquid problems reach the screening lane,
without weakening inspection, adding a worse queue, or turning the rule into a
long legal explanation?

## Synopsis

An airport checkpoint keeps stalling because non-compliant carry-on liquids are discovered late at the X-ray belt, minor per incident but costly in aggregate.

cases.title: When The Old Advice Was To Stay Small
cases.body:
# When The Old Advice Was To Stay Small

prev_id: null

## Context

For years, a common software warning was to avoid boiling the ocean. Do not
rebuild the package registry. Do not rethink source control. Do not build a new
team communications system. Do not create a new mobile distribution layer. Do
not make your own benchmark ecosystem. Pick a narrow wedge, ship the smallest
thing, and avoid infrastructure traps.

That warning was often correct. The hard part of many systems was not the first
prototype. It was the long tail: package metadata, weird Git states, sync
conflicts, device policies, user trust, security reviews, migration paths,
documentation, support, and compatibility with old workflows. Big rewrites
failed because coordination and maintenance cost overwhelmed the insight.

The constraint may be moving. AI agents can generate code, inspect large diffs,
run checks, produce migration scaffolds, research prior art, maintain repeated
tasks, and operate disposable environments. They do not remove complexity, but
they can lower the cost of attacking systems that were previously too broad for
small teams.

That does not mean every large idea is newly good. Some oceans are still oceans.
The bottleneck may move from code to trust, distribution, governance,
regulation, data rights, human attention, or who owns a shared state transition.
The old advice becomes dangerous only when it hides a changed constraint.

The question is what should be reconsidered now. Which formerly absurd systems
problems are newly approachable because implementation and coordination cost
fell? Which are still blocked by non-code constraints? How should a builder tell
the difference between a once-impossible project whose bottleneck moved and a
classic overreach with better tools?

## Synopsis

Conventional software advice warned against rebuilding infrastructure (do not boil the ocean); the case probes whether that caution still holds once the hard part of these systems has shifted.

cases.title: When the Crashes Don't Come
cases.body:
# When the Crashes Don't Come

prev_id: null

## Context

As of June 22, 2026, this is a Tesla case. Waymo proves driverless ride-hailing can
operate, but its geofenced fleet model and prohibitive cost structure are not the
discontinuity being tested here.

The signals are no longer theoretical. Tesla's Robotaxi service is live in
limited areas of Austin, Dallas, and Houston. Texas now requires commercial
automated-vehicle operators to hold state authorization, turning a permissive
market into a regulated commercial channel. In California, Tesla Robotaxi LLC now
appears on the DMV testing-with-driver permit list; California has not granted
Tesla driverless deployment, but the barrier has moved into the regulatory
pipeline. Globally, Tesla says Full Self-Driving (Supervised) has already rolled
out in the United States, Canada, Mexico, Puerto Rico, Australia, New Zealand,
South Korea, the Netherlands, Lithuania, Estonia, Denmark, and Belgium.
China is a messier but important signal: Tesla announced FSD Supervised
availability there in May 2026, while full-market regulatory clearance and
fleet-wide availability remain less clean.

The premise of this case is that Tesla has solved full self-driving. The question is what happens if the bottleneck is no longer invention, but permission and adoption, across an existing vehicle platform.

Suppose Tesla's model starts to produce the result everyone wanted: fewer crashes caused by human driving error en masse, without rebuilding the fleet city by city. The safety story is not ambiguous. Fewer people are killed, fewer people are injured, fewer families get the phone call no one wants.

The public conversation does not get very far. The visible story is that rides
get much cheaper and taxi, Uber, and Lyft drivers lose work. Investors talk about
ride-hailing economics. Labor reporters talk about drivers. Local officials talk
about street use, airport pickups, and whether the service is allowed. For most
people, that is the whole frame.

Almost nobody treats crash reduction as an economic demand shock. A crash is not only a bad event; it is also the beginning of a long chain of paid work, supply lines, and public cost. Someone files a claim. Someone tows the vehicle. Someone repairs, stores, salvages, auctions, litigates, bills, treats, rehabilitates, or administers the consequences. Some organizations are paid per incident. Others maintain capacity because severe crashes arrive often enough to justify it.

No one is trying to preserve crashes. That is not the question. The question is
what has to adapt when a massive recurring source of harm falls away, especially
if most of the exposed systems are not yet thinking of themselves as exposed.

### The Situation

The rollout is uneven and the architecture matters. A software-driven fleet that
can be enabled across many existing vehicles changes the timing, geography, and
scale of the shock. Texas shows the public-service side of the Tesla rollout.
California shows the permission boundary: testing with a driver is not driverless
deployment. The larger question is what happens if permission turns a working
autonomy stack into broad deployment before adjacent institutions have named the
risk.

Liability may also shift: a remaining crash might involve a fleet operator,
manufacturer, software provider, sensor supplier, owner, passenger, or road
authority rather than a single human driver.

The remaining crashes may not look like today's average crashes either. There may be
fewer of them, but some individual incidents could be more expensive because modern
vehicles carry sensors, calibration requirements, software records, and specialized
parts.

The obvious labor story is only one surface. A lower-crash world may also touch:

- insurers, agents, reinsurers, adjusters, and claims organizations
- collision repair, parts, towing, storage, salvage, and auction businesses
- injury lawyers, courts, settlement services, and billing intermediaries
- emergency medical services, trauma centers, rehabilitation providers, and
disability systems
- public agencies and households that absorb crash costs, lost work, disruption,
and administrative burden

Traffic stops, fines, forfeiture, driver licensing, and license-as-ID may also
change under autonomy, but they are not the center of this case unless the link runs
through crash harm.

### Decision Point

You are looking past the visible cheap-rides-and-driver-displacement story.

What should the dependency map include, and which crash-linked businesses,
public agencies, medical systems, legal systems, and household costs need the
closest attention if human-caused crash volume falls sharply?

## Synopsis

With Tesla robotaxi service live in parts of Texas, the case examines what happens to the large economy built around vehicle crashes as autonomy makes them rarer.

cases.title: The Patchwork Arrival
cases.body:
# The Patchwork Arrival

prev_id: null

## Context

Tesla-scale autonomy does not arrive everywhere at once.

As of June 22, 2026, Tesla Robotaxi service is available only in limited areas of
Austin, Dallas, and Houston. Texas has an enforceable commercial authorization
program for automated vehicles operating without a human driver. California has
Tesla Robotaxi LLC on its testing-with-driver permit list, but Tesla is not listed
for California driverless testing or deployment. Globally, Tesla says FSD
(Supervised) has rolled out in the United States, Canada, Mexico, Puerto Rico,
Australia, New Zealand, South Korea, the Netherlands, Lithuania, Estonia, Denmark,
and Belgium. That supervised product still requires an attentive driver and is not
the same legal thing as a driverless robotaxi.

The result is a strange adoption pattern. In one place, autonomy is a thing people
can summon with an app. In another, it is a permit application. In another, it is a
driver-assistance subscription. In another, it is still a headline, a stock argument,
or something people assume is fake because they have never seen it work.

This matters because the consequences do not wait for national consensus. If one
metro gets reliable autonomous rides and another does not, their labor markets,
airport access, parking demand, insurance experience, crash rates, court volume,
and household car decisions may begin to diverge. If one country accepts supervised
FSD and another blocks it, consumer expectations and local data loops diverge. If
one state lets commercial driverless service operate and another keeps it trapped in
testing, the boundary itself becomes economically important.

The belief gap may be as important as the coverage gap. People living inside the
service area can update from experience. Everyone else updates through clips,
crashes, jokes, earnings calls, political fights, and secondhand arguments. That
creates room for real-world arbitrage: workers, regulators, investors, insurers,
dealers, cities, airports, and households may act as if the shift is still distant
after it is already routine somewhere else.

The split can also run internationally. Rich, mapped, regulator-ready metros may get
the technology first. But the opposite is possible in specific places if a country
with lighter legacy constraints decides that road safety, logistics cost, or weak
transit makes fast adoption worth it. The arrival pattern is not automatically fair,
linear, or predictable.

### Decision Point

You are comparing places that will experience autonomy at different times and under
different legal meanings.

Which gaps matter most: lived experience, regulation, vehicle fleet, weather,
mapping, infrastructure, labor politics, insurance, or public trust? What changes at
the boundary between places where autonomy is ordinary and places where it is still
treated as speculation?

## Synopsis

Tesla-scale autonomy arrives as a patchwork, live in limited Texas areas and gated differently across states and countries, rather than everywhere at once.

problem.body:
Tesla-scale autonomy does not arrive everywhere at once.

As of June 22, 2026, Tesla Robotaxi service is available only in limited areas of
Austin, Dallas, and Houston. Texas has an enforceable commercial authorization
program for automated vehicles operating without a human driver. California has
Tesla Robotaxi LLC on its testing-with-driver permit list, but Tesla is not listed
for California driverless testing or deployment. Globally, Tesla says FSD
(Supervised) has rolled out in the United States, Canada, Mexico, Puerto Rico,
Australia, New Zealand, South Korea, the Netherlands, Lithuania, Estonia, Denmark,
and Belgium. That supervised product still requires an attentive driver and is not
the same legal thing as a driverless robotaxi.

The result is a strange adoption pattern. In one place, autonomy is a thing people
can summon with an app. In another, it is a permit application. In another, it is a
driver-assistance subscription. In another, it is still a headline, a stock argument,
or something people assume is fake because they have never seen it work.

This matters because the consequences do not wait for national consensus. If one
metro gets reliable autonomous rides and another does not, their labor markets,
airport access, parking demand, insurance experience, crash rates, court volume,
and household car decisions may begin to diverge. If one country accepts supervised
FSD and another blocks it, consumer expectations and local data loops diverge. If
one state lets commercial driverless service operate and another keeps it trapped in
testing, the boundary itself becomes economically important.

The belief gap may be as important as the coverage gap. People living inside the
service area can update from experience. Everyone else updates through clips,
crashes, jokes, earnings calls, political fights, and secondhand arguments. That
creates room for real-world arbitrage: workers, regulators, investors, insurers,
dealers, cities, airports, and households may act as if the shift is still distant
after it is already routine somewhere else.

The split can also run internationally. Rich, mapped, regulator-ready metros may get
the technology first. But the opposite is possible in specific places if a country
with lighter legacy constraints decides that road safety, logistics cost, or weak
transit makes fast adoption worth it. The arrival pattern is not automatically fair,
linear, or predictable.

### Decision Point

You are comparing places that will experience autonomy at different times and under
different legal meanings.

Which gaps matter most: lived experience, regulation, vehicle fleet, weather,
mapping, infrastructure, labor politics, insurance, or public trust? What changes at
the boundary between places where autonomy is ordinary and places where it is still
treated as speculation?

cases.title: The Missing Driver In The Traffic Stop
cases.body:
# The Missing Driver In The Traffic Stop

prev_id: null

## Context

Traffic enforcement assumes a human driver. Someone speeds, rolls a stop, drifts
across a lane, looks down at a phone, drives impaired, forgets paperwork, ignores a
registration deadline, misses an inspection, or gives an officer a reason to pull
the car over.

Tesla-scale autonomy tests that assumption directly. Texas now requires commercial
automated-vehicle authorization for passenger or cargo service without a human
driver. California has moved in the same direction from the other side: its new AV
rules create notices of noncompliance when an autonomous vehicle commits a moving
violation, and the notice goes to the manufacturer or operator rather than to a human
driver who is not there.

That sounds like a technical enforcement update. It is bigger than that.

Traffic stops are one of the most common police-citizen contacts in the United
States. In 2022, about 49 million U.S. residents age 16 or older reported contact
with police; among police-initiated contacts, drivers in traffic stops were the
largest category. A stop can remain a simple warning or ticket. It can also become a
search, a warrant check, a vehicle tow, a forfeiture event, a license suspension, an
insurance surcharge, or a court appearance.

The revenue story is uneven. Traffic fines are not a large share of state and local
revenue in aggregate, but they matter intensely in some places. Some towns, courts,
vendors, and law practices are built around high-volume traffic enforcement. Fuel
taxes are already under pressure from EVs and efficient vehicles, and road-usage
charging programs in states such as Oregon and Utah show the replacement logic:
charge by mile instead of by gallon or by incident.

Autonomous vehicles do not make state power disappear. They change where it can
attach. A human driver can be stopped, cited, questioned, searched, licensed,
suspended, tested, and priced. A fleet vehicle can be logged, geofenced, remotely
contacted, restricted by permit, or charged by mile. California's new AV rules point
there: operators must respond to first responders, obey emergency geofences, report
more data, and can face operating restrictions.

The familiar public concern is whether robotaxis will block traffic, pick up at the
airport, or kill driving jobs. The less familiar question is what happens to a legal
and fiscal apparatus built around fallible individual drivers when the driver is no
longer the operating unit.

### Decision Point

You are advising a state that expects autonomous service to grow county by county.

Which enforcement, court, identity, revenue, and civil-liberties dependencies should
it examine before the human traffic stop starts to shrink?

## Synopsis

Traffic enforcement assumes a human driver to cite; driverless autonomy removes that driver from the traffic stop.

cases.title: When The Driver Stops Being The Cost
cases.body:
# When The Driver Stops Being The Cost

prev_id: null

## Context

Tesla's Robotaxi rollout is still limited, and supervised FSD still requires an
attentive driver. For this case, accept the stronger premise: the technology is good
enough that legality, operating design, and adoption are now the bottlenecks.

The public conversation sees the ride-hailing surface. If rides get cheaper, taxi,
Uber, and Lyft drivers lose work. That is the part everyone can understand.

The less visible change is that driver attention and driver labor have been basic
prices in the transportation system. A person has to stay awake on a long drive. A
truck driver has legal rest limits. A commuter burns attention twice a day. A parent
or caregiver becomes a driver by default. A local delivery business routes around
labor cost, schedule reliability, loading time, parking, and insurance. A short
flight competes not with highway miles, but with a door-to-door ordeal: airport
access, security, boarding, delays, pickup, and the wasted fragments around each
step.

There are already exposed surfaces before autonomy becomes universal. Heavy and
tractor-trailer truck drivers held about 2.2 million U.S. jobs in 2024. Delivery
truck drivers and driver/sales workers held about 1.5 million. Taxi drivers, shuttle
drivers, and chauffeurs held about 448,000, and the taxi category includes
ride-hailing drivers. These are not all hit at the same time, and not all driving
tasks are easy. But the labor base is large enough that a slow transition is still a
major transition.

Long-distance travel has its own pressure points. Cars already dominate many trips
under 500 miles; air starts winning only after distance gets long enough to overcome
the airport penalty. Short domestic flights have also been weakening for years. A
cheap, sleepable, door-to-door autonomous ride does not need to beat a plane in the
air. It only needs to beat the whole trip.

Freight and roadside businesses face a different version of the same question.
Today's roadside economy is built for human drivers who need fuel, bathrooms, meals,
showers, sleep, parking, repairs, compliance stops, and downtime. Driverless freight
still needs energy, maintenance, routing, and staging, but it does not need the same
human stopping pattern.

Then there is the daily hour. The average commute is still close to half an hour
each way for many workers. If the trip can become sleep, work, entertainment, or
errand time, the boundary around a labor market changes. Places that were too far
for daily life may become tolerable. Some cities may stretch. Others may get more
concentrated. The car becomes less like a steering task and more like a moving room.

### Decision Point

You are looking beyond cheap rides.

If driver attention and driver labor stop being the binding costs, which travel,
freight, labor, and geography systems change first, and which changes only arrive
after the rollout becomes reliable enough for people to plan their lives around it?

## Synopsis

Granting that the technology works well enough, the case looks at what changes when the driver stops being the cost of mobility, beyond the visible ride-hailing layer.

cases.title: The Car You Stop Owning
cases.body:
# The Car You Stop Owning

prev_id: null

## Context

Americans do not just use cars. They finance them, insure them, trade them in,
repair them, park them, register them, borrow against them, build garages for them,
and organize daily life around having one nearby.

Tesla-scale autonomy puts pressure on that ownership pattern. Robotaxi service is
already live in limited Texas areas. Supervised FSD is spreading through owned Tesla
vehicles in multiple countries, while still requiring an attentive driver. The
combined signal is not just that rides may get cheaper. It is that one company is
trying to make autonomy part of a vehicle platform that can be sold, subscribed to,
updated, and possibly used as a service asset.

The U.S. ownership stack is huge. Auto-loan balances were about $1.69 trillion in
Q1 2026. Franchised light-vehicle dealers sold 16.2 million light-duty vehicles in
2025, generated more than $1.3 trillion in sales, and wrote more than 276 million
repair orders. Dealerships are not only showrooms; they are finance desks, trade-in
funnels, local employers, service businesses, warranty channels, political actors,
and beneficiaries of state franchise laws.

The private car also has a utilization problem. It is expensive and sits unused for
most of the day. That made sense when the alternative was waiting, walking, taking
transit that did not match the trip, or paying another human to drive. If a reliable
autonomous ride is always near, the household car has to justify itself differently.

There are at least three live possibilities:

- households keep owning cars because autonomy makes the owned car more useful
- households own fewer cars because cheap service covers ordinary trips
- ownership mutates because a household vehicle can work as a paid robotaxi when
  the owner is not using it

Those paths hit different institutions. A pure service model pressures retail unit
sales, used-car residuals, auto lending, parking, fuel retail, and household
insurance. A household robotaxi model may preserve financing but change what is
being financed: not a depreciating convenience asset, but an income-producing
machine with different risk, wear, tax, repair, and liability questions.

The visible story is again too small. People can understand cheaper rides and driver
job loss. Far fewer are asking what happens to the car as the default household
asset.

### Decision Point

You are looking at the ownership economy before the market settles.

Which parts depend on households buying and maintaining privately used vehicles, and
which parts can survive if mobility shifts toward fleets, subscriptions, or
income-producing owned cars?

## Synopsis

American daily life is organized around owning a car (financing, insurance, repair, parking); the case examines how autonomy pressures that whole ownership pattern.

cases.title: The Switch Under The Fleet
cases.body:
# The Switch Under The Fleet

prev_id: null

## Context

As of June 22, 2026, treat this as a Tesla case. The premise is not that every
road, weather condition, regulator, or consumer has already yielded. The premise is
that Tesla-scale full self-driving works well enough that the binding question has
moved from invention to permission, rollout, and adoption.

The current signals are concrete. Tesla's Robotaxi service is operating in limited
areas of Austin, Dallas, and Houston, Texas, using Model Y vehicles and a dedicated
Robotaxi app. Texas now requires commercial automated-vehicle operators to hold a
state authorization for vehicles operating without a human driver. California has
Tesla Robotaxi LLC on its testing-with-driver permit list, but not on its driverless
testing or deployment lists. California's new autonomous-vehicle rules also create a
staged path through safety-driver testing, driverless testing, and deployment, with
traffic-law accountability aimed at the operator rather than a missing human driver.

Tesla also says Full Self-Driving (Supervised) has rolled out beyond North America:
Australia, New Zealand, South Korea, the Netherlands, Lithuania, Estonia, Denmark,
and Belgium are listed alongside the United States, Canada, Mexico, and Puerto Rico.
That supervised product still requires an attentive driver. It does not make a
consumer Tesla legally autonomous. But it matters because the same company is pushing
two related gates at once: supervised capability spreading through owned cars, and
commercial driverless service beginning in limited Texas markets.

That makes the discontinuity different from a small robotaxi fleet. The hard
question is what happens if autonomy can be distributed through an installed vehicle
base, a shared software stack, and over-the-air updates rather than through slow,
city-by-city fleet construction.

The public story is still shallow. Most people see cheaper rides, displaced Uber and
taxi drivers, and a fight over whether the cars should be allowed on certain streets.
That is not wrong. It is just the smallest visible surface.

Driving is not one industry. It is a load-bearing behavior under insurance, repair,
freight, public revenue, identity, land use, airport access, policing, commute
geography, household balance sheets, and local labor markets. Some parts depend on
people driving badly. Some depend on people owning cars. Some depend on people being
stuck behind the wheel. Some depend on the state having millions of individual
drivers to license, stop, ticket, insure, and tax.

If the Tesla premise holds, the first wave will look mundane: a few service areas,
permit fights, pricing experiments, cautious expansion, bad headlines after
individual incidents, and arguments about safety statistics. The deeper question is
which systems are quietly built on the assumption that human driving remains normal.

### Decision Point

You are advising someone who accepts the Tesla premise before most institutions
around the road economy have updated.

Which dependent systems deserve separate investigation first, and how would you tell
which ones merely get cheaper, which ones lose the event they were built around,
and which ones move from individual drivers to fleet-scale operators?

## Synopsis

Granting that Tesla-scale full self-driving works well enough, the binding question shifts from invention to permission, rollout, and adoption.

cases.title: GLP-1s In The Grocery Basket
cases.body:
# GLP-1s In The Grocery Basket

prev_id: null

## Context

In 2026, GLP-1 drugs are no longer only a diabetes-care story or a celebrity
weight-loss story. A KFF poll from late 2025 found that 12% of U.S. adults were
currently taking a GLP-1 drug such as Ozempic, Wegovy, Mounjaro, or Zepbound,
and 18% had taken one at some point.

The grocery aisle is starting to show it. A household panel study published in
2026 found that households with a GLP-1 user cut grocery spending by about 5%
within six months of adoption. The reductions were larger in higher-income
households and heavier in calorie-dense processed categories. Savory snacks,
sweet baked goods, candy, sugary drinks, and limited-service restaurant visits
all look more exposed than basic staples. A few categories, such as yogurt and
some protein-forward foods, moved the other way.

For a packaged-food company, this does not arrive as one clean signal. The same
weekly report can show fewer impulse units, stronger interest in protein,
smaller portion packs, private-label pressure, normal inflation noise, and a
retailer asking for a new "GLP-1 friendly" shelf story. The consumer may be the
person taking the drug, but the basket belongs to a household. One person eating
less at night can change what gets bought for everyone else. If the person stops
taking the drug, some spending may drift back.

The public conversation is still mostly about weight loss, side effects, drug
pricing, and whether food companies can launch healthier snacks. Inside a
category team, the harder question is different. A salty-snack line, a candy
aisle, a frozen-meal brand, and a grocer's front-end impulse rack do not have the
same exposure, margin structure, or replacement occasions.

The team has to decide what it is seeing while the market is still moving. Is
this a temporary diet wave, a product-mix shift, a household-level demand change,
or something else? Which lost occasions can be replaced, which only shrink, and
which disappear?

## Synopsis

With roughly 12% of US adults on a GLP-1 drug, household grocery spending (especially impulse categories) is starting to show measurable decline.

cases.title: Heinz Ketchup Bottle Authenticity
cases.body:
# Heinz Ketchup Bottle Authenticity

prev_id: null

## Context

Heinz has a table-service problem in some restaurants and street-food venues.
The customer sees the familiar Heinz bottle, but the brand no longer controls
what is inside it. A venue can buy Heinz bottles, refill them with cheaper
ketchup, and keep presenting the bottle as if nothing changed.

The deception is awkward to police. Contracts and distributor pressure may deter
some venues, but they do not help the customer at the table. Taste is too slow
and too subjective. A phone-based check would add friction to a condiment. A
heavy tamper-proof package would be expensive and wasteful for a bottle that
often lives in ordinary restaurant service.

Heinz needs a cheap, familiar package-level change that makes substitution
harder to hide during normal use.

What could the bottle do, without asking the customer to scan, read, or perform
an authentication ritual?

## Synopsis

Venues refill branded Heinz bottles with cheaper ketchup, and the brand cannot control or prove what is actually inside the bottle at the table.

cases.title: Houston Baggage Claim Complaints
cases.body:
# Houston Baggage Claim Complaints

prev_id: null
## Context

An airport has a baggage-claim complaint problem. Passengers on a route keep
saying the bags take too long.

Operations improves the mechanical process, but the complaints do not go away.
The remaining delay is not mostly a broken belt or lazy handling. Passengers are
getting from the arrival gate to the carousel very quickly, then standing still
while the ordinary baggage process catches up.

From the airport's view, bag delivery is near the practical floor. From the
passenger's view, the experience is an idle wait in a public room after a long
flight. The airport needs fewer complaints, but it cannot justify a major
baggage-system rebuild for a process that is already close to normal.

What could the airport change so the same baggage timing feels less like a
failure, without pretending the bags are arriving materially faster?

## Synopsis

Baggage-claim complaints persist after operational fixes because passengers reach the carousel quickly, then stand waiting for the normal process to catch up.

problem.body:
An airport has a baggage-claim complaint problem. Passengers on a route keep
saying the bags take too long.

Operations improves the mechanical process, but the complaints do not go away.
The remaining delay is not mostly a broken belt or lazy handling. Passengers are
getting from the arrival gate to the carousel very quickly, then standing still
while the ordinary baggage process catches up.

From the airport's view, bag delivery is near the practical floor. From the
passenger's view, the experience is an idle wait in a public room after a long
flight. The airport needs fewer complaints, but it cannot justify a major
baggage-system rebuild for a process that is already close to normal.

What could the airport change so the same baggage timing feels less like a
failure, without pretending the bags are arriving materially faster?

cases.title: The Rock Star's Drone Problem
cases.body:
# The Rock Star's Drone Problem

prev_id: null

## Context

A famous rock star owned a superyacht and used it the way very rich, very famous
people sometimes use superyachts: as a private world offshore, away from hotel
balconies, public streets, restaurant tables, and ordinary lines of sight.

The yacht was not just transport or status. It was where he could host parties,
bring people aboard, and behave as if land-based consequences were far away. That
assumption had started to fail from above. Paparazzi were using drones launched
from shore or nearby vessels to circle the yacht and film open decks before the
people onboard could do much about it.

The problem was not an abstract dislike of drones. A drone could capture photographs
that created a personal scandal. The owner did not want those photographs, but he
also did not want the party to stop every time someone saw a dot in the sky. He had
bought privacy, not a floating panic room.

### The Situation

The yacht was operating near busy coastal waters, not in an empty military test
range. Other vessels, beaches, port infrastructure, aircraft, and public
communications systems could be nearby. The captain, crew, and security team were
available, but they were not the real customer. Their job was to make the owner's
problem disappear without turning his night into a visible security operation.

Some anti-drone systems that might be acceptable offshore or in a controlled
environment could be illegal or reckless in port-adjacent waters. The yacht could
often know a drone was coming before the owner could see it, but detection alone
did not decide what the crew should do next.

The obvious responses all had problems:

- A projectile, net gun, or interceptor could bring the drone down, but nobody
could control where it would fall or what it might hit.
- Radio-frequency jamming might affect more than the target drone. The industry had
stories of port-side interference causing serious trouble far outside the yacht.
- Some drones could lose contact, keep recording, and return home with footage
anyway.
- A visible confrontation could attract more attention than the original intrusion.
- Repeated alarms could train the owner and guests to ignore the warning or ruin
the night even when no usable photographs were taken.

The owner wanted the same thing that made the problem hard: keep the party feeling
private, effortless, and uninterrupted while making sure a drone did not leave with
the wrong photographs.

### Decision Point

You have been asked to recommend a response before the next night onboard. What
would you tell the owner's team to do when paparazzi drones approach the yacht?

Your proposal should account for detection, timing, crew behavior, legal exposure,
false alarms, and the owner's desire to keep the night going. It can combine
technology, procedure, crew training, or changes to how exposed areas are used.

## Synopsis

A celebrity's offshore yacht relied on being out of ordinary sight; that privacy assumption fails from above as paparazzi deploy drones.

cases.title: The $2 Million Internet Problem
cases.body:
# The $2 Million Internet Problem

prev_id: null

## Context

A charter guest paying roughly $2 million a week does not think of the yacht as a
remote site. They expect their phone, laptop, video calls, feeds, streams, games,
and work sessions to behave the way they do at home or in a hotel suite.

At sea, that expectation sits on moving infrastructure. The yacht can be near shore
with cellular coverage in the morning, outside useful cellular range that night,
behind a mast or superstructure at the wrong moment, in a crowded marina, or on a
route where satellite availability, local licensing, hardware state, and weather
all matter. A guest only sees the visible failure: a frozen call, a stalled upload,
a stream that buffers, a payment session that dies, or a crew member asking them
to reconnect.

Starlink changed the economics and performance of yacht connectivity. A yacht can
now get low-latency satellite internet with smaller terminals and far lower monthly
cost than the old VSAT-only world. Many yachts still keep VSAT, add cellular near
shore, and use routers that can fail over or bond multiple links.

That improves the odds. It does not make continuity automatic. Every added path
adds hardware, antennas, accounts, coverage assumptions, switching logic,
cybersecurity exposure, and another thing that can fail. In a true no-bearer
moment, buying one more link may not help at all.

The captain and owner want a yacht where high-value users feel continuous service,
not a technical diagram with more subscriptions on it.

What would you recommend for the yacht's connectivity architecture, onboard
operations, and guest-facing promise?

## Synopsis

A high-paying charter guest expects home-grade connectivity, but the yacht's link rides moving infrastructure at sea that drifts in and out of coverage.

cases.title: The Swim-Up Boarding
cases.body:
# The Swim-Up Boarding

prev_id: null

## Context

A large private yacht had crew onboard, a night watch, access controls, and CCTV.
It still had a basic security failure.

An intruder entered the water from shore, swam out to the vessel, used a tender as
a step, climbed aboard, and reached the interior before anyone stopped him. The
tender mattered because it turned the yacht's height above the water from a barrier
into a ladder.

The embarrassment was not that the yacht had no security. It had the visible parts
of security: cameras, crew, procedures, and people watching the obvious approaches.
The missed approach was lower, slower, and quieter. It came through the water at
night, where reflections, darkness, marine life, debris, cleaning work, small craft,
and ordinary crew movement can all create noise.

The owner and guests do not want the yacht to feel like a prison. The captain does
not want a security system that screams all night because a turtle, floating bag,
or crew member appears in the wrong place. The security team needs a warning early
enough to act before someone touches the tender, hull, swim platform, or guest
spaces.

What would you recommend for detecting and responding to a swimmer or diver
approaching the yacht before they board?

## Synopsis

Despite crew, a night watch, and CCTV, an intruder swam out from shore, used the tender as a step, and reached the yacht's interior; the waterline perimeter was unguarded.

cases.title: Loft Insulation Adoption
cases.body:
# Loft Insulation Adoption

prev_id: null

## Context

UK households are under-adopting loft insulation even when the economics look
good. The offer is easy to defend on paper: less heat loss, lower energy use,
more comfort, and a long-lived home improvement.

The program's problem is not just awareness. Some households express interest.
Some book audits. Some accept that insulation would be sensible. Then the journey
breaks before installation is completed.

That matters because this is not a clean online purchase. It is a domestic
service visit inside a private, awkward part of the house. The household has to
let workers in, prepare the space, deal with disruption, and fit the job into
normal life. A campaign can look persuasive at the leaflet or booking stage and
still fail at the point where the home has to be made ready.

What should the program change if completed installations, not stated interest,
are the real measure?

## Synopsis

UK households under-adopt loft insulation despite favorable economics; interest exists, but the journey breaks down before installation completes.

cases.title: London Underground Passenger Map
cases.body:
# London Underground Passenger Map

prev_id: null

## Context

By the early 1930s, London's Underground had become too complex for the old style
of passenger map. The lines spread far outside the central city, stations were
unevenly spaced, and the dense central network was exactly where riders needed
the most clarity.

A geographically faithful map had an obvious virtue: it showed where things
really were. But it also created practical problems. Central stations crowded
together. Outer branches consumed space. The shape of the city competed with the
shape of the rail system. Riders trying to answer ordinary journey questions
could be forced to parse street geography they did not need.

Most passengers were not using the Underground map to walk between stations or
measure exact surface distance. They needed to know which line to take, where to
change, and how stops connected.

What kind of map would make the railway easier to use, even if it made a
geographer uncomfortable?

## Synopsis

By the 1930s the London Underground was too sprawling for a geographically faithful map; the dense centre, where riders needed clarity most, was unreadable.

cases.title: When Mobile Builders Cannot Ship
cases.body:
# When Mobile Builders Cannot Ship

prev_id: null

## Context

Mobile used to attract builders who modified the platform itself. Custom ROMs,
experimental Android distributions, alternative interface ideas, and new mobile
operating systems gave ambitious developers a place to work close to the device.
The feedback loop was imperfect, but the imagination was wide: change the
platform, flash the device, share the build, and see what users did.

That loop narrowed. App stores, signing chains, device locks, review processes,
payments, banking-app compatibility, malware defenses, and hardware support
turned distribution into the binding constraint. A small team can still write an
app, launcher, marketplace, ROM, or operating system layer. Getting it onto
ordinary user devices, keeping it updated, preserving trust, and supporting
enough hardware is much harder.

Live alternatives prove both demand and friction. Independent Android app
repositories can distribute open apps, but installation and updates still run
through platform prompts and policy pressure. Custom Android systems can be
excellent, but they often narrow to specific devices for security and support.
European regulation has opened alternative iOS distribution paths, but those
paths still include authorization, notarization, regional limits, settings flows,
and fees.

The build side is getting cheaper. A smaller team can now generate code, test
compatibility layers, write tooling, and maintain surface area that would have
been too expensive before. But cheaper building exposes the non-code constraint:
getting software onto devices and keeping it there.

The question is where an ambitious mobile builder should intervene. Is the
opening a new mobile operating system, a better ROM, a sideloading helper, an app
store, developer tooling, device support, payment flow, policy fight, or some
combination that changes the user path?

## Synopsis

Mobile once welcomed builders who modified the platform itself, but app stores, signing chains, and device locks have closed off that distribution loop.

cases.title: The Thread That Has To Wake Back Up
cases.body:
# The Thread That Has To Wake Back Up

prev_id: null

## Context

Work chat optimizes for sending messages. That makes sense during live
coordination, but it breaks down when important work happens over hours or days.
A decision, blocker, investigation, or agent result may land deep in an old
thread. The people who need it may never see it. The conversation exists, but it
does not reliably return to the top of the team's attention.

The old failure mode was human memory. The newer failure mode includes agents.
Agents can summarize, reply, execute tasks, and produce updates, but a channel
feed does not tell them which conversations are still alive, which replies close
a loop, which nested comment changes priority, or who owns a stale unresolved
thread.

Several products point at the gap. Topic-first chat makes threads easier to
navigate. Async tools make conversations less frantic. Project tools attach
comments to durable work objects. Incumbent chat products are adding summaries,
canvases, lists, and agents. These are meaningful patches, but they do not
always change the primitive. The message stream often remains the source of
truth.

The question is where attention should attach. Should the important unit be a
channel, a thread, a task, a document, a notification, or a separate object that
borrows from all of them? Which old conversations should return to view, which
should stay quiet, and how should an agent know the difference?

## Synopsis

Work chat is built for sending messages, so decisions and agent results land deep in old threads and never reliably resurface to the team's attention.

cases.title: The Benchmark Only Practitioners Can Write
cases.body:
# The Benchmark Only Practitioners Can Write

prev_id: null

## Context

Large AI systems improve against the measurements that matter. Public exams,
coding tasks, web tasks, and desktop-control tasks all turn messy capability into
numbers that labs can compare. Once a measurement becomes visible, it can change
what teams optimize.

The strongest failures are often not in general benchmark suites. They live in
practitioner worlds: obscure command-line workflows, domain-specific grammar,
specialized visual reasoning, messy repository history, realistic web tasks, or
desktop routines that experts perform without thinking. A model can sound fluent
and still fail the task.

Practitioner benchmarks turn that hidden pain into a runnable environment. They
can test specialized vocabulary, spatial reasoning, stateful tool use, command
sequencing, messy repositories, and operational judgment that broad exams miss.
Software issue benchmarks, web environments, and desktop-control suites show the
same direction at larger scale: capability has to be measured where the work
actually happens.

There is a trap. A benchmark is not pressure just because it exists. It has to be
trusted, cheap enough to run, hard enough to differentiate systems, hard to game,
and visible to the people who choose models. Once it succeeds, it can decay:
tasks leak, models train against it, scaffolds overfit it, and the remaining
errors stop representing the field.

The question is how practitioners should turn lived failure into durable
measurement. Which tasks deserve public prestige? Which tasks should stay held
out? How often should the suite refresh? Who adjudicates ambiguous cases? What
keeps the benchmark from becoming another saturated scoreboard?

## Synopsis

AI systems improve against visible benchmarks, but the hardest failures live in obscure practitioner workflows that no public benchmark captures.

cases.title: The Pull Request No One Has Time To Read
cases.body:
# The Pull Request No One Has Time To Read

prev_id: null

## Context

For a decade the scarce resource in software was writing the code. Now an
engineer paired with agents can open dozens of correct-looking changes an hour,
and a team of such engineers can produce more diff in a morning than the old
review process absorbed in a week. The constraint did not disappear. It moved.
The bottleneck is no longer authorship; it is integration: review, merge, and
the human attention required to trust a change before it ships.

This is where slop accumulates. When the queue grows faster than anyone can read
it, the rational move under pressure is to approve on vibes, trust the green
check, and merge. Each rubber-stamped change is individually plausible and
collectively corrosive: subtle bugs, dead branches, drifting conventions, and
eroded architecture compounding at machine speed. The danger is not one bad PR.
It is a system that ships faster than it can form judgment, where quality decays
precisely because everyone is moving quickly and no one is the bottleneck that
used to catch things.

The obvious answer is to point more agents at the problem: agents reviewing
agents, automated approvals, bots that gate the merge queue. But agents
reviewing agents have correlated blind spots. They share training, share style,
and can converge on changes that look reviewed without being understood. An
approval with no one accountable for it is ceremony. If a reviewer cannot be
wrong in a way that costs them something, the review does not carry trust.

Partial solutions already exist. Merge queues and stacked-diff tools raise
serialization throughput. CI, type gates, and property and mutation testing turn
some judgment into machine checks. AI review bots comment, summarize, and flag
risk. Each speeds one stage. None of them answers who is allowed to be wrong, or
which changes still require a human to look.

The question is where trust should attach when humans can no longer read every
diff. Is the reviewable unit still the pull request, or should humans own the
invariants, contracts, and risk classes while agents own the line-level pass?
What must a human still hold to keep the system from slopping itself into decay,
and what can safely be delegated to machines reviewing machines?

## Synopsis

Engineers paired with agents now produce changes far faster than review and merge can absorb them, so the bottleneck moves to integration and quality decays into slop unless trust is relocated off the per-diff human read.

cases.title: Sealed Facility / Staged Crisis
cases.body:
# Sealed Facility / Staged Crisis

prev_id: null

## Context

A coordinated criminal team seizes a sealed, high-value corporate facility under the cover of a political hostage crisis. The team is armed, organized, and operating on a precise internal timeline. Their stated demands — political, ideological — fit a well-understood threat profile. Official response mobilizes immediately: law enforcement, professional negotiators, and federal authorities escalate through standard protocols.

Everything inside the facility is locked down. One person is not accounted for. A civilian professional who happened to be inside at the moment of the seizure is now physically present, undetected, and unknown to all parties — the response commanders, the negotiators, and the criminal team itself.

The official response escalates correctly by every standard measure: perimeter secured, negotiators engaged, federal jurisdiction assumed, internal access systematically ceded in exchange for hostage cooperation. None of it neutralizes the threat. The criminal plan advances in step with the official response, as if the two are in coordination.

The civilian, with no chain of command and no resources beyond what the building contains, begins acting independently.

A criminal team stages a political hostage crisis inside a sealed corporate facility to conceal a financial theft; the official response escalates through every standard protocol while the civilian insider — unknown to all parties — acts alone.

cases.title: Singapore Rail Peak Crowding
cases.body:
# Singapore Rail Peak Crowding

prev_id: null

## Context

Singapore's rail system has a morning peak problem. The trains and platforms are
most stressed in a narrow commute window, while nearby time bands have more spare
capacity. Building new rail capacity is slow and expensive, and the pressure is
not evenly distributed across every line, station, or group of riders.

Some commuters have no real flexibility. School, caregiving, shift work,
connections, and employer expectations fix their travel time. Others could move
earlier or later if the tradeoff were visible enough and the rule were simple.

The rail authority has fare gates, stored-value and account-based payment, travel
history, station-level data, and the ability to communicate time-window rules.
But any change has to be legible, automatic, adjustable, and fair enough not to
punish people whose schedules cannot move.

What could shift enough flexible trips out of the most crowded window to make the
system feel better, without building new track?

## Synopsis

Singapore's rail system is stressed in a narrow morning-peak window while adjacent time bands sit underused; some commuters can shift their travel, many cannot.

cases.title: Source Control Beyond The Repository Flag
cases.body:
# Source Control Beyond The Repository Flag

prev_id: null

## Context

Git hosting usually treats visibility as a repository property. A project is
public or private, and teams add branch rules, review rules, and access roles on
top. That model works when the important question is who can read or write the
whole codebase.

Modern software work has smaller secrecy boundaries. An open project may need a
private security fix before disclosure. A maintainer may need a private branch
inside a public repository. A company may want public source with private
deployment overlays. A team may want generated or experimental agent patches to
be reviewable without immediately exposing the entire work stream.

The work shape has also changed. AI agents can produce many speculative edits,
partial stacks, and snapshots. Human branch ceremony becomes expensive when the
working state changes faster than the review queue. Newer tools show the
pressure: stacked-change systems make dependent work easier to review, while
snapshot-oriented systems reduce the cost of saving unfinished state.

But visibility is not just a user-interface flag. If hidden code exists, search,
review, tests, caches, logs, artifacts, dependency metadata, and agent context
can all leak it. A temporary private fork for a security advisory is useful, but
it is a special-purpose shelter, not the normal shape of source control.

The question is which object should carry trust and visibility. Is the repository
still the right boundary, or should policies attach to changes, files, snapshots,
branches, and disclosure state? What would it take to run review and automation
over hidden code without making secrecy a cosmetic layer?

## Synopsis

Git hosting treats visibility as a whole-repository property, but modern work needs finer secrecy boundaries, like a private security fix inside a public project.

cases.title: If Launch Stops Being The Expensive Part
cases.body:
# If Launch Stops Being The Expensive Part

prev_id: null

## Context

Starship is still a development program, not a routine freight line. That is the
first fact to keep in view. SpaceX says the vehicle is designed to carry more
than 100 metric tonnes to orbit in a fully reusable configuration, far above
Falcon 9's published LEO payload. But the lower-cost regime depends on things
that are not proven by a single flight: reuse of both stages, rapid
refurbishment, high cadence, payload integration, range operations, reliability,
and regulatory clearance.

The current signals are real but incomplete. On May 22, 2026, SpaceX flew
Starship Flight 12, the first flight of the V3 vehicle, from Starbase in Texas.
The upper stage reached space and deployed 20 Starlink simulators plus two
modified Starlink satellites, but an in-space engine relight was skipped after an
engine loss, and the booster return created a Federal Aviation Administration
mishap investigation. Separately, the FAA's environmental review for Starbase
analyzed operations up to 25 annual Starship/Super Heavy launches and landings,
which is permission for a higher-cadence path, not evidence that the cadence
already exists.

The market's usual story is destinations: Mars, the Moon, NASA, Starlink, maybe
point-to-point travel. A payload planner sees a different question. If mass to
orbit gets cheap enough and frequent enough, some designs stop trying to be
ultralight, some payloads can be physically larger, some servicing or replacement
cycles change, and some infrastructure that looked absurd under Falcon pricing
can be tested.

That does not mean every space fantasy becomes a business. Cheap launch can move
the bottleneck somewhere else: payload manufacturing, radiation tolerance,
thermal management, on-orbit power, spectrum, debris rules, ground stations,
propellant supply, environmental approvals, insurance, customer demand, or the
ability to integrate and fly payloads as fast as the rocket can launch.

The problem is to reason from the cost curve without pretending the future has
already arrived. What changes first if Starship reaches reliable high-cadence
reuse? What remains scarce even if kilograms to orbit become much cheaper? Which
payloads, suppliers, and operating constraints matter only after launch cost
stops dominating every spreadsheet?

## Synopsis

Starship is still developmental, but the case asks what opens up if its reusable design makes launch stop being the expensive part of reaching orbit.

cases.title: The Development Folder That Is Not A Folder
cases.body:
# The Development Folder That Is Not A Folder

prev_id: null

## Context

Developers now move between laptops, Linux boxes, remote workstations, cloud
sandboxes, NAS storage, and agent runtimes. The code may be the same project,
but the state is rarely the same. One machine has the branch, another has the
uncommitted edit, another has the right dependency cache, another has the
credential reference, and another has the agent session that understands what
just happened.

Plain file sync sounds like the obvious answer. Consumer sync tools can make a
folder appear on multiple machines. But active development folders are not normal
folders. They contain repository metadata, generated files, lockfiles, caches,
machine-specific paths, editor state, local overlays, build outputs, credential
references, and worktree state. Syncing all bytes can create conflicts or leak
state. Syncing too little leaves every new machine half broken.

Git also does not fully solve the problem. It captures committed history, and
large-repo tooling can lazily hydrate objects. Remote development environments
can provide a persistent machine in the cloud. Secret managers can inject values
at run time. Each piece is useful, but the user still has to decide which machine
owns the dirty work and which parts of the environment follow.

Agents raise the cost of this gap. A clone that takes minutes, a missing local
setting, or a stale branch is no longer just a developer annoyance. It becomes
setup latency multiplied across many short-lived workers. The faster the agents
become, the more expensive environment friction looks.

The question is what should be synchronized. Is the product a folder of bytes, a
Git repository, a remote workspace, a handoff protocol, or something else? What
should follow the work, what should stay machine-local, and who decides when two
machines disagree?

## Synopsis

Developers work across laptops, remote boxes, sandboxes, and agent runtimes where the code matches but the state (branches, edits, caches, sessions) does not.

cases.title: VanMoof Shipping Damage
cases.body:
# VanMoof Shipping Damage

prev_id: null

## Context

VanMoof is selling expensive bikes directly to customers. That means a large,
awkward, high-value product has to survive an ordinary third-party shipping
chain before the customer ever rides it.

Too many bikes arrive damaged. Each damaged shipment creates replacement cost,
support load, delay, waste, and a bad first ownership moment. The company can add
more protective material, use heavier crates, or pay for more careful shipping,
but each of those responses hurts cost, weight, waste, and scale.

VanMoof does not control every handler, depot, truck, stack, or loading decision.
Once the box enters the freight system, it is one large cardboard box among many.

What could VanMoof change so bikes arrive damaged less often, without making each
shipment materially heavier or operationally special?

## Synopsis

VanMoof ships expensive bikes direct to customers through ordinary carriers, and too many arrive damaged, creating cost and a poor first-ownership moment.

cases.title: When the Crashes Don't Come
cases.body:
# When the Crashes Don't Come

prev_id: null
## Context

As of June 22, 2026, this is a Tesla case. Waymo proves driverless ride-hailing can
operate, but its geofenced fleet model and prohibitive cost structure are not the
discontinuity being tested here.

The signals are no longer theoretical. Tesla's Robotaxi service is live in
limited areas of Austin, Dallas, and Houston. Texas now requires commercial
automated-vehicle operators to hold state authorization, turning a permissive
market into a regulated commercial channel. In California, Tesla Robotaxi LLC now
appears on the DMV testing-with-driver permit list; California has not granted
Tesla driverless deployment, but the barrier has moved into the regulatory
pipeline. Globally, Tesla says Full Self-Driving (Supervised) has already rolled
out in the United States, Canada, Mexico, Puerto Rico, Australia, New Zealand,
South Korea, the Netherlands, Lithuania, Estonia, Denmark, and Belgium.
China is a messier but important signal: Tesla announced FSD Supervised
availability there in May 2026, while full-market regulatory clearance and
fleet-wide availability remain less clean.

The premise of this case is that Tesla has solved full self-driving. The question is what happens if the bottleneck is no longer invention, but permission and adoption, across an existing vehicle platform.

Suppose Tesla's model starts to produce the result everyone wanted: fewer crashes caused by human driving error en masse, without rebuilding the fleet city by city. The safety story is not ambiguous. Fewer people are killed, fewer people are injured, fewer families get the phone call no one wants.

The public conversation does not get very far. The visible story is that rides
get much cheaper and taxi, Uber, and Lyft drivers lose work. Investors talk about
ride-hailing economics. Labor reporters talk about drivers. Local officials talk
about street use, airport pickups, and whether the service is allowed. For most
people, that is the whole frame.

Almost nobody treats crash reduction as an economic demand shock. A crash is not only a bad event; it is also the beginning of a long chain of paid work, supply lines, and public cost. Someone files a claim. Someone tows the vehicle. Someone repairs, stores, salvages, auctions, litigates, bills, treats, rehabilitates, or administers the consequences. Some organizations are paid per incident. Others maintain capacity because severe crashes arrive often enough to justify it.

No one is trying to preserve crashes. That is not the question. The question is
what has to adapt when a massive recurring source of harm falls away, especially
if most of the exposed systems are not yet thinking of themselves as exposed.

## The Situation

The rollout is uneven and the architecture matters. A software-driven fleet that
can be enabled across many existing vehicles changes the timing, geography, and
scale of the shock. Texas shows the public-service side of the Tesla rollout.
California shows the permission boundary: testing with a driver is not driverless
deployment. The larger question is what happens if permission turns a working
autonomy stack into broad deployment before adjacent institutions have named the
risk.

Liability may also shift: a remaining crash might involve a fleet operator,
manufacturer, software provider, sensor supplier, owner, passenger, or road
authority rather than a single human driver.

The remaining crashes may not look like today's average crashes either. There may be
fewer of them, but some individual incidents could be more expensive because modern
vehicles carry sensors, calibration requirements, software records, and specialized
parts.

The obvious labor story is only one surface. A lower-crash world may also touch:

- insurers, agents, reinsurers, adjusters, and claims organizations
- collision repair, parts, towing, storage, salvage, and auction businesses
- injury lawyers, courts, settlement services, and billing intermediaries
- emergency medical services, trauma centers, rehabilitation providers, and
disability systems
- public agencies and households that absorb crash costs, lost work, disruption,
and administrative burden

Traffic stops, fines, forfeiture, driver licensing, and license-as-ID may also
change under autonomy, but they are not the center of this case unless the link runs
through crash harm.

## Decision Point

You are looking past the visible cheap-rides-and-driver-displacement story.

What should the dependency map include, and which crash-linked businesses,
public agencies, medical systems, legal systems, and household costs need the
closest attention if human-caused crash volume falls sharply?

## Synopsis

With Tesla robotaxi service live in parts of Texas, the case examines what happens to the large economy built around vehicle crashes as autonomy makes them rarer.

# Actuarial Collapse in Specialty Auto Reinsurance

prev_id: [[when-the-crashes-dont-come-575845a4]]

## Trace

### Case study · synopsis

With Tesla robotaxi service live in parts of Texas, the case examines what happens to the large economy built around vehicle crashes as autonomy makes them rarer.

## Discovery

### Finding 1

Texas commercial authorization requires state-backed risk pools for automated operators → [[financial-derivatives-7a1c4e90#^authorization-pool-mandate]]

### Finding 2

CA DMV testing permits reveal liability gaps in deployment pipelines → [[financial-derivatives-7a1c4e90#^authorization-pool-mandate]]

### Finding 3

Global FSD Supervised rollout across 10+ jurisdictions compresses loss-data collection cycles → [[financial-derivatives-7a1c4e90#^freq-trigger]]

## Growth — Problem recovery

### Surface complaint

Investors focus on ride-hailing unit economics and driver displacement.

### Deleted assumption

Insurance-linked securities and reinsurance pools price risk based on stable human-error frequency distributions.

### Hidden variable

Crash volume acts as the primary trigger and liquidity engine for catastrophe bonds, specialty auto captives, and secondary liability markets.

### Actual problem

Capital markets face basis risk when autonomy decouples vehicle value from traditional Poisson loss triggers, causing sudden repricing events and liquidity crunches in fleet risk transfer.

### Candidate response

Transition catastrophe modeling to AI-fleet stress testing with real-time telemetry triggers and establish a sovereign backstop for autonomy-specific tail risk.

### Skin in the Game

- Specialty auto reinsurers
- Captive insurance underwriters
- Reinsurance brokers
- Fleet risk managers

### Sprouts

- Telemetry-backed parametric payouts
- Dynamic solvency ratios tied to fleet autonomy levels
- Sovereign tail-risk liquidity facilities

3 evidence item(s); 3 causal markers; 0 hedge(s).

cases.title: White Castle Real Estate Leverage
cases.body:
# White Castle Real Estate Leverage

prev_id: null

## Context

White Castle is expanding a chain of small, standardized hamburger restaurants
in the 1920s and 1930s. The company wants each restaurant to feel clean,
recognizable, and repeatable, not like a one-off roadside lunch counter.

Real estate creates a hostage problem. A good location matters, but buying every
parcel ties up capital and slows expansion. Leasing keeps expansion lighter, but
the restaurant investment becomes vulnerable to the landlord. Once White Castle
has built a recognizable store on a leased parcel, a rent hike or non-renewal can
strand capital that is physically attached to someone else's land.

Harder lease negotiation may help one site. It does not remove the structural
dependence. The company needs the brand consistency of a real building and the
capital discipline of a lease-heavy rollout.

How could White Castle make expansion less vulnerable to any single landlord's
parcel, using materials and operations available at the time?

## Synopsis

White Castle's 1920s-30s expansion of standardized restaurants faces a real-estate hostage problem: buying ties up capital, while leasing exposes the investment to landlords.
